comment_text.sn10.12:1.2: Another spirit of place, Āḷavaka’s story (also at Snp 1.10) is perhaps the most beloved of all the yakkha conversions. He is provided with an extensive backstory in the Pali commentaries, and another quite different story in a Chinese Dharmapada (T 211, pp. 575–609). | Āḷavī was a town between Sāvatthī and Rājagaha.
comment_text.sn10.12:9.2: Following Cone’s Dictionary of Pali, Pali gharamesin probably stands for Sanskrit gṛhamedhin, an “householder of discernment”, a man of mature years who has completed his education.
comment_text.sn10.12:10.4: Readings are uncertain. Chinese parallels at SA2 325 and SA 1326 support dama or danta here rather than dhamma. DN 30:1.6.2 has both, and elsewhere we find both dhamma and dama commonly used alongside sacca.

root_text.sn10.12:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā āḷaviyaṁ viharati āḷavakassa yakkhassa bhavane.
root_text.sn10.12:1.3: Atha kho āḷavako yakkho yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.12:1.8: Dutiyampi kho āḷavako yakkho bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.12:1.13: Tatiyampi kho āḷavako yakkho bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.12:1.18: Catutthampi kho āḷavako yakkho bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn10.12:1.23: Sace me na byākarissasi, cittaṁ vā te khipissāmi, hadayaṁ vā te phālessāmi, pādesu vā gahetvā pāragaṅgāya khipissāmī”ti.
root_text.sn10.12:1.24: “Na khvāhaṁ taṁ, āvuso, passāmi sadevake loke samārake sabrahmake sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiyā pajāya sadevamanussāya, yo me cittaṁ vā khipeyya hadayaṁ vā phāleyya, pādesu vā gahetvā pāragaṅgāya khipeyya.
root_text.sn10.12:1.25: Api ca tvaṁ, āvuso, puccha yadā kaṅkhasī”ti (…).

translation_text.sn10.12:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Āḷavī in the haunt of the native spirit Āḷavaka.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.3: Then the native spirit Āḷavaka went up to the Buddha, and said to him:
translation_text.sn10.12:1.5: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.7: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.8: And for a second time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn10.12:1.10: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.12: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.13: And for a third time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn10.12:1.15: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went out.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.17: Saying, “All right, respectable sir,” the Buddha went in.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.18: And for a fourth time the native spirit Āḷavaka said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn10.12:1.20: “No, respectable sir, I won’t get out.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.22: “I will ask you a question, ascetic.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.23: If you don’t answer me, I’ll drive you insane, or explode your heart, or grab you by the feet and throw you to the far shore of the Ganges!”
translation_text.sn10.12:1.24: “I don’t see anyone in this world with its gods, Māras, and Divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its gods and humans who could do that to me.
translation_text.sn10.12:1.25: But anyway, ask what you wish.”
translation_text.sn10.12:2.2: What brings happiness when practiced well?
translation_text.sn10.12:2.3: What’s the sweetest taste of all?
translation_text.sn10.12:2.4: The one they say has the best life: how do they live?”
translation_text.sn10.12:3.1: “Faith here is a person’s best wealth.
translation_text.sn10.12:3.2: The teaching brings happiness when practiced well.
translation_text.sn10.12:3.3: Truth is the sweetest taste of all.
translation_text.sn10.12:3.4: The one they say has the best life lives by wisdom.”
translation_text.sn10.12:4.2: How do you cross the deluge?
translation_text.sn10.12:4.3: How do you get over suffering?
translation_text.sn10.12:5.3: By energy you get past suffering,
translation_text.sn10.12:6.3: How do you get a good reputation?
translation_text.sn10.12:6.4: How do you hold on to friends?
translation_text.sn10.12:6.5: How do the departed not grieve
translation_text.sn10.12:6.6: when passing from this world to the next?”
translation_text.sn10.12:7.2: gains wisdom by wanting to learn,
translation_text.sn10.12:7.3: having faith in the perfected ones,
translation_text.sn10.12:7.4: and the teaching for becoming extinguished.
translation_text.sn10.12:8.2: and working hard you earn wealth.
translation_text.sn10.12:8.3: Truthfulness wins you a good reputation.
translation_text.sn10.12:8.4: You hold on to friends by giving.
translation_text.sn10.12:8.5: That’s how the departed do not grieve
translation_text.sn10.12:8.6: when passing from this world to the next.
translation_text.sn10.12:9.3: does not grieve after passing away:
translation_text.sn10.12:10.1: Go ahead, ask others as well,
translation_text.sn10.12:10.2: there are many ascetics and brahmins.
translation_text.sn10.12:10.3: See whether anything better is found
translation_text.sn10.12:10.4: than truth, principle, generosity, and patience.”
translation_text.sn10.12:11.4: what’s good for the next life.
translation_text.sn10.12:12.1: It was truly for my benefit
translation_text.sn10.12:12.2: that the Buddha came to stay at Āḷavī.
translation_text.sn10.12:12.4: where a gift is very fruitful.
translation_text.sn10.12:13.2: village to village, town to town,
translation_text.sn10.12:13.4: and the clear rightness of the teaching!”
translation_text.sn10.12:14.5: The Linked Discourses with Native Spirits are complete.

variant_text.sn10.12:1.25: (…) → (atha kho āḷavako yakkho bhagavantaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi.) (bj, pts2ed)

reference_text.sn10.12:1.1: cck15.275, dr15.297, ms12S1_1512, msdiv246, pts-vp-pli1ed1.214, pts-vp-pli2ed1.461, sya15.315, vri23.248
